summary: Bryan Cantrill recounts the origin story of Fishworks, a project he co-founded with Mike Shapiro at Sun Microsystems in 2005 to build an integrated NAS appliance. Despite Sun having all the necessary components (ZFS, DTrace, NFS), they had zero market share in NAS storage. After pitching the idea to Sun's CTO Greg Papadopoulos and getting unexpected immediate support, the team built what became the Sun Storage 7000 series, featuring DTrace-based analytics that visually answer questions about storage performance.
key_points: Sun had revolutionary technologies but zero NAS market share due to organizational barriers
The infrastructure needed for special-purpose appliances turned out to be general-purpose across different applications
DTrace-based analytics were designed to answer questions in terms of system abstractions rather than implementation details
Visual interfaces can serve as a 'force multiplier' for underlying instrumentation technology
money_quotes: Despite having invented network attached storage with NFS in 1983, and despite having the necessary components to efficiently serve files built into the system, and despite having absolutely killer technologies like ZFS and DTrace, Sun had no share – none – of the NAS market.

At that instant, we realized that the NAS problem was but one example of a larger problem, and that the infrastructure to build fully-integrated, special-purpose systems was itself general-purpose across those special purposes!
Greg rose from his chair and exclaimed, 'let's do it!' Mike and I were caught a bit flat-footed; we had expected a much safer, more traditional answer – like 'let's commission a task force!'
With analytics, we sought to harness the great power of DTrace: its ability to answer ad hoc questions that are phrased in terms of the system's abstractions instead of its implementation.
We have come up with ways to visualize and interact with data that actually function as a force multiplier for the underlying instrumentation technology.
key_insight: Having best-in-class technology components is insufficient without organizational willingness to integrate them into complete products that solve real customer problems.
